result.output:
# Lex - Vietnamese Telex input method

The specification of the Lex program, a Vietnamese Telex input method.

## Vietnamese Telex Rules

### Diacritics

#### Empty

- Any literal alphabet character has empty diacritic by default.
- Only a specific number of characters have other diacritic and it must follow the rules for those characters.

#### Circumflex

- Valid for: `A` (Â), `E` (Ê), `O` (Ô) and their lower cases.

#### Breve

- Valid for: `A` (Ă) and its lower case.

#### Horn

- Valid for: `O` (Ơ), `U` (Ư) and their lower cases.

#### Bar

- Valid for: `D` (Đ) and its lower case.

#### Apply rules

- Trigger when user input the same character (ignore case) with the immediate previous character, e.g. existing `A`, input `a`, result `Â`.
- The existing character keep its case.
- New diacritic must be valid for applying.
- Only apply diacritic when the existing span doesn't have any diacritic.

#### Override rules

- New valid diacritic can override existing valid diacritic of the same base, e.g. existing _ă_ (aw), input _a_ will
  override to _â_ (aa). The same apply for the opposite, existing _â_, input _w_ will override to
  _ă_.

#### Cancel rules

- The new diacritic must be the same with the existing diacritic to cancel the existing one, e.g. existing `Ô`, input `o`, result `Oo`. 
- The existing character and the new character keep their own case, as the above example.
- Switch to literal input for subsequent inputs after cancelling.
- When the user backspaces pass the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.

#### Auto-fill rules

### Tones

#### Level

#### Rising

#### Falling

#### Dipping-rising

#### Rising-glottalized

#### Falling-glottalized

#### Apply rules

#### Override rules

#### Reposition rules

**Evaluating character handling**

I’m thinking that describing "any literal alphabet character has empty diacritic" might be too broad, especially if I’m considering the basic ASCII A-Z. I realize that the Vietnamese alphabet excludes certain letters like F, J, W, and Z, but the input method can still manage those literals. It says that a literal alphabet character has an empty diacritic by default, so that simplifies things a bit, I guess!

content.text:
For the “valid diacritics” inventory, you did not miss any Vietnamese base-letter diacritic cases, assuming tones are handled separately.

Correct valid non-tone diacritics:

| Diacritic | Valid bases | Vietnamese letters |
|---|---|---|
| Circumflex | `A`, `E`, `O` | `Â/â`, `Ê/ê`, `Ô/ô` |
| Breve | `A` | `Ă/ă` |
| Horn | `O`, `U` | `Ơ/ơ`, `Ư/ư` |
| Bar / stroke | `D` | `Đ/đ` |

No other base letters take these marks in modern Vietnamese Quốc ngữ. In particular:

- `I`, `Y` do not take circumflex/breve/horn/bar.
- `U` takes horn only, not breve/circumflex.
- `E` takes circumflex only.
- `O` takes circumflex or horn, not breve.
- `A` takes circumflex or breve, not horn.
- `D` takes stroke only.
- Tone marks are separate and can stack on Vietnamese vowels, including `ă`, `â`, `ê`, `ô`, `ơ`, `ư`.

One wording issue: “Bar” is understandable, but Vietnamese `Đ/đ` is usually called **stroke** or **crossbar**, not a vowel diacritic. If the spec uses “diacritic” broadly for all Telex transformations, it is fine; otherwise rename `Bar` to `Stroke`.

Suggested small clarification:

> Non-tone letter diacritics are valid only for these base letters: circumflex on `A/E/O`, breve on `A`, horn on `O/U`, and stroke on `D`, including lowercase forms. Tone marks are handled separately and may combine with Vietnamese vowels.
Good. I updated the file. Continue review the diacritic "apply rules", the rules is written in general way that must be correct for all diacritic.

# Lex - Vietnamese Telex input method

The specification of the Lex program, a Vietnamese Telex input method.

## Vietnamese Telex Rules

### Diacritics

Diacritics let a letter have many variant. We only focus on letter diacritics in this section.

#### Empty

- Any literal alphabet character has empty diacritic by default.
- Only a specific number of characters have other diacritic and it must follow the rules for those characters.

#### Circumflex

- Valid for: `A` (Â), `E` (Ê), `O` (Ô) and their lower cases.

#### Breve

- Valid for: `A` (Ă) and its lower case.

#### Horn

- Valid for: `O` (Ơ), `U` (Ư) and their lower cases.

#### Bar

- Valid for: `D` (Đ) and its lower case.

#### Apply rules

- Only apply diacritic when the immediate previous character doesn't have any diacritic (empty).
- _Circumflex_, _bar_ are triggered when user inputs the same character (ignore case) with the existing character when that character is valid for the applying diacritic.
  - `Aa` -> `Â`, `Ee` -> `Ê`, `Oo` -> `Ô`.
  - `Dd` -> `Đ`.
- _Breve_, _horn_ are triggered when user input `W`, `w` after the character when that character is valid for the applying diacritic. Otherwise, treat `W`, `w` literally.
  - `Aw` -> `Ă`.
  - `Ow` -> `Ơ`, `Uw` -> `Ư`.
- The existing character keep its case.
- Treat out of scope characters as literal.

#### Override rules

- New valid diacritic can override existing valid diacritic of the same base, e.g. existing _ă_ (aw), input _a_ will
  override to _â_ (aa). The same apply for the opposite, existing _â_, input _w_ will override to
  _ă_.

#### Cancel rules

- The new diacritic must be the same with the existing diacritic to cancel the existing one, e.g. existing `Ô`, input `o`, result `Oo`. 
- The existing character and the new character keep their own case, as the above example.
- Switch to literal input for subsequent inputs after cancelling.
- When the user backspaces pass the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.

#### Auto-fill rules

### Tones

#### Level

#### Rising

#### Falling

#### Dipping-rising

#### Rising-glottalized

#### Falling-glottalized

#### Apply rules

#### Override rules

#### Reposition rules

content.thinking:
**Considering character input**

I'm analyzing the input for `D` to see if it turns into `Đ`. It seems good so far, but I’m wondering if there might be a potential issue with having characters that could be the same as existing precomposed characters. Since the first bullet requires the immediate previous character to be empty, it looks like that won't be a problem after all. But I'm curious—could Vietnamese input methods combine tones over already toned vowels?

content.text:
The diacritic **apply rules** are mostly correct and general enough for all letter diacritics. The main cases are covered:

- Same-letter trigger:
  - `a/a` → circumflex on `A/E/O`.
  - `d/d` → stroke/bar on `D`.
- `w/W` trigger:
  - `a + w` → breve.
  - `o/u + w` → horn.
- Invalid combinations fall back to literal input.
- Existing character case is preserved.
- Application only happens when the existing character has empty letter diacritic.

Cases to explicitly verify or clarify:

1. **Mixed-case triggers should be valid**

   Your text says “ignore case,” so these should all apply:

   - `Aa`, `AA`, `aA`, `aa`
   - `Dd`, `DD`, `dD`, `dd`

   Result should preserve the first/existing character’s case:

   - `aA` → `â`
   - `Aa` → `Â`
   - `dD` → `đ`
   - `Dd` → `Đ`

2. **`W/w` trigger case should be ignored**

   You wrote `W`, `w`, so these should apply:

   - `Aw`, `AW` → `Ă`
   - `aw`, `aW` → `ă`
   - `Ow`, `OW` → `Ơ`
   - `ow`, `oW` → `ơ`
   - `Uw`, `UW` → `Ư`
   - `uw`, `uW` → `ư`

3. **Invalid same-letter cases should remain literal**

   Worth making explicit because “same character” applies only to valid bases:

   - `Bb` → `Bb`
   - `Ii` → `Ii`
   - `Yy` → `Yy`
   - `Uu` → `Uu`, because `U` has no circumflex rule.
   - `Aa` applies, but `Aw` applies differently.

4. **Invalid `w` cases should remain literal**

   These should not apply any diacritic:

   - `Ew` → `Ew`
   - `Iw` → `Iw`
   - `Yw` → `Yw`
   - `Dw` → `Dw`
   - `Bw` → `Bw`

5. **“Empty diacritic” may need one precision**

   The rule says:

   > Only apply diacritic when the immediate previous character doesn't have any diacritic (empty).

   Since this section excludes tones, clarify whether “empty” means **no letter diacritic only**, or **no letter diacritic and no tone**.

   For Vietnamese behavior, usually letter diacritics and tones can coexist, so the better wording may be:

   > Only apply a letter diacritic when the immediate previous character has no existing letter diacritic.

   This avoids accidentally blocking cases like applying `w` to a toned `a` if tones are processed before letter diacritics.

Suggested tightened version:

```md
#### Apply rules

- Only apply a letter diacritic when the immediate previous character has no existing letter diacritic.
- _Circumflex_ and _bar_ are triggered when the user inputs the same character as the immediate previous character, ignoring case, and that character is valid for the target diacritic.
  - `Aa`, `AA` -> `Â`; `aa`, `aA` -> `â`.
  - `Ee` -> `Ê`, `Oo` -> `Ô`.
  - `Dd`, `DD` -> `Đ`; `dd`, `dD` -> `đ`.
- _Breve_ and _horn_ are triggered when the user inputs `W` or `w` after a character that is valid for the target diacritic.
  - `Aw`, `AW` -> `Ă`; `aw`, `aW` -> `ă`.
  - `Ow` -> `Ơ`, `ow` -> `ơ`.
  - `Uw` -> `Ư`, `uw` -> `ư`.
- The existing character keeps its case.
- If no apply rule matches, treat the new input as literal.
```

Main correction: prefer **“no existing letter diacritic”** over **“doesn't have any diacritic”** if tones are separate and allowed to combine.
All good, no need to go further on "apply rules". I updated the file. Focus on reviewing the Diacritic "override rules", there will be more work for you.

# Lex - Vietnamese Telex input method

The specification of the Lex program, a Vietnamese Telex input method.

## Vietnamese Telex Rules

### Diacritics

Diacritics let a letter have many variant. We only focus on letter diacritics in this section. Tones have seperated section.

#### Empty

- Any literal alphabet character has empty diacritic by default.
- Only a specific number of characters have other diacritic and it must follow the rules for those characters.

#### Circumflex

- Valid for: `A` (Â), `E` (Ê), `O` (Ô) and their lower cases.

#### Breve

- Valid for: `A` (Ă) and its lower case.

#### Horn

- Valid for: `O` (Ơ), `U` (Ư) and their lower cases.

#### Bar

- Valid for: `D` (Đ) and its lower case.

#### Apply rules

- Only apply diacritic when the immediate previous character doesn't have any diacritic (empty).
- _Circumflex_, _bar_ are triggered when user inputs the same character (ignore case) with the existing character when that character is valid for the applying diacritic.
  - `Aa` -> `Â`, `Ee` -> `Ê`, `Oo` -> `Ô`.
  - `Dd` -> `Đ`.
- _Breve_, _horn_ are triggered when user input `W`, `w` after the character when that character is valid for the applying diacritic. Otherwise, treat `W`, `w` literally.
  - `Aw` -> `Ă`.
  - `Ow` -> `Ơ`, `Uw` -> `Ư`.
- The existing character keep its case, tone.
- Treat out of scope characters as literal.

#### Override rules

- New valid diacritic can override existing valid diacritic of the same base character.
  - `Ăa` -> `Â`, `Âw` -> `Ă`.
  - `Ôw` -> `Ơ`, `Ơo` -> `Ô`.
- Only `A`, `O` have override rules because they have more than one possible litter diacritic.
- Keep tone untouched.
- The existing character keep its case, tone.
- Treat out of scope characters as literal.

#### Cancel rules

- The new diacritic must be the same with the existing diacritic to cancel the existing one, e.g. existing `Ô`, input `o`, result `Oo`. 
- The existing character and the new character keep their own case, as the above example.
- Switch to literal input for subsequent inputs after cancelling.
- When the user backspaces pass the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.

#### Auto-fill rules

### Tones

#### Level

#### Rising

#### Falling

#### Dipping-rising

#### Rising-glottalized

#### Falling-glottalized

#### Apply rules

#### Override rules

#### Reposition rules

content.thinking:
**Clarifying character rules**

I'm thinking about the rules for existing characters like Đ and d, particularly regarding cancellations. I need to consider using a consistent tone for related characters like horn or circumflex over O. The base characters for Ơ/Ô are O, and for Ă/Â they are A. It's important to deliver a concise answer, but I might also want to note any grammar typos, such as "seperated" or "litter." My focus is on providing the answer without editing further.

content.text:
Override rules are conceptually correct. The valid override cases are exactly:

| Existing letter diacritic | New input | Result |
|---|---:|---|
| `Ă/ă` breve `A` | `a/A` | `Â/â` |
| `Â/â` circumflex `A` | `w/W` | `Ă/ă` |
| `Ô/ô` circumflex `O` | `w/W` | `Ơ/ơ` |
| `Ơ/ơ` horn `O` | `o/O` | `Ô/ô` |

So this statement is correct:

> Only `A`, `O` have override rules because they have more than one possible letter diacritic.

Cases you should ensure the spec makes clear:

1. **Override only applies when the new diacritic is different from the existing one**

   These are override:

   - `Ăa` → `Â`
   - `Âw` → `Ă`
   - `Ôw` → `Ơ`
   - `Ơo` → `Ô`

   These are **not** override; they belong to cancel rules:

   - `Âa`
   - `Ăw`
   - `Ôo`
   - `Ơw`

2. **Input case should be ignored**

   If apply rules ignore trigger case, override should too:

   - `Ăa`, `ĂA` → `Â`
   - `ăa`, `ăA` → `â`
   - `Âw`, `ÂW` → `Ă`
   - `âw`, `âW` → `ă`
   - `Ơo`, `ƠO` → `Ô`
   - `ơo`, `ơO` → `ô`

3. **Existing character case is preserved**

   This is already stated and correct:

   - `Ăa` → `Â`
   - `ăa` → `â`
   - `Ôw` → `Ơ`
   - `ôw` → `ơ`

4. **Tone should transfer to the replacement letter**

   “Keep tone untouched” is right, but worth thinking of examples:

   - `Ắa` → `Ấ`
   - `Ấw` → `Ắ`
   - `Ổw` → `Ở`
   - `Ờo` → `Ồ`

   Meaning: same base letter case, new letter diacritic, same tone.

5. **Invalid override attempts should become literal**

   These should not override:

   - `Êw` → `Êw`
   - `Ưo` → `Ưo`
   - `Đa` → `Đa`
   - `Ăe` → `Ăe`
   - `Ơa` → `Ơa`

Suggested tightened wording:

```md
#### Override rules

- A new valid letter diacritic can override an existing different letter diacritic of the same base character.
  - `Ăa`, `ĂA` -> `Â`; `ăa`, `ăA` -> `â`.
  - `Âw`, `ÂW` -> `Ă`; `âw`, `âW` -> `ă`.
  - `Ôw`, `ÔW` -> `Ơ`; `ôw`, `ôW` -> `ơ`.
  - `Ơo`, `ƠO` -> `Ô`; `ơo`, `ơO` -> `ô`.
- Only `A` and `O` have override rules because they have more than one possible letter diacritic.
- If the new input would produce the same letter diacritic as the existing one, use cancel rules instead.
- The existing character keeps its case and tone.
- If no override rule matches, treat the new input as literal.
```

Minor typo: `litter diacritic` should be `letter diacritic`.

I updated the minor typo. Now focus on the Diacritic "cancel rules". Review my specification to find any gap. Consider the it in the whole picture of the Diacritics section. It's OK for incomplete example, focus on the rules.

result.output:
# Lex - Vietnamese Telex input method

The specification of the Lex program, a Vietnamese Telex input method.

## Vietnamese Telex Rules

### Diacritics

Diacritics let a letter have many variant. We only focus on letter diacritics in this section. Tones
have seperated section.

#### Empty

- Any literal alphabet character has empty diacritic by default.
- Only a specific number of characters have other diacritic and it must follow the rules for those
  characters.

#### Circumflex

- Valid for: `A` (Â), `E` (Ê), `O` (Ô) and their lower cases.

#### Breve

- Valid for: `A` (Ă) and its lower case.

#### Horn

- Valid for: `O` (Ơ), `U` (Ư) and their lower cases.

#### Bar

- Valid for: `D` (Đ) and its lower case.

#### Apply rules

- Only apply diacritic when the immediate previous character doesn't have any diacritic (empty).
- _Circumflex_, _bar_ are triggered when user inputs the same character (ignore case) with the
  existing character when that character is valid for the applying diacritic.
  - `Aa` -> `Â`, `Ee` -> `Ê`, `Oo` -> `Ô`.
  - `Dd` -> `Đ`.
- _Breve_, _horn_ are triggered when user input `W`, `w` after the character when that character is
  valid for the applying diacritic. Otherwise, treat `W`, `w` literally.
  - `Aw` -> `Ă`.
  - `Ow` -> `Ơ`, `Uw` -> `Ư`.
- The existing character keep its case, tone.
- Treat out of scope characters as literal.

#### Override rules

- New valid diacritic can override existing valid diacritic of the same base character.
  - `Ăa` -> `Â`, `Âw` -> `Ă`.
  - `Ôw` -> `Ơ`, `Ơo` -> `Ô`.
- Only `A`, `O` have override rules because they have more than one possible letter diacritic.
- Keep tone untouched.
- The existing character keep its case, tone.
- Treat out of scope characters as literal.

#### Cancel rules

- The cancellation is triggered if and only if user inputs the character (ignore case) that would
  produce the existing diacritic for the same base character of the immediate previous character.
- The existing character keeps its case, tone, but the diacritic is removed.
  - Circumflex: `Âa` -> `Aa`, `Ốo` -> `Óo`... and so on for other cases of the same kind.
  - Breve: `Ăw` -> `Aw`.
  - Horn: `Ưw` -> `Uw`... and so on for other cases of the same kind.
  - Bar: `Đd` -> `Dd`, `đD` -> `dD`.
- The new character is literal, keeps its case, no tone.
- Switch to literal from the new character position and the following characters. Literal input
  remains until the process is reset (usually when starting new word).
  - Existing `Â`, input `aa`, result `Aaa`.
- When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.

#### Auto-fill rules

### Tones

_(the tones specification is not ready)_

#### Level

#### Rising

#### Falling

#### Dipping-rising

#### Rising-glottalized

#### Falling-glottalized

#### Apply rules

#### Override rules

#### Reposition rules

content.thinking:
**Clarifying diacritic rules**

I'm thinking about how multiple diacritics work and how inputs may cancel or override each other. For example, when you add an "a" to "Â," it cancels the circumflex, but adding "w" creates a breve instead. I also want to clarify the concept of "reset"—it's vague right now and could benefit from precise definitions about when it occurs. Should it trigger on spaces or punctuation? Lastly, I need to correct "pass" to "past" for clarity.

**Examining character input rules**

I'm thinking about how backspacing affects character input, especially in relation to Vietnamese diacritics. If I backspace past the literal point, should it restore Vietnamese input? For instance, if I have an existing input of "Â" followed by "aa," and I delete the last "a," what happens? Should we switch back to Vietnamese input? Also, if I delete the cancellation character, does the "A" retain its diacritic? I might need to include a note about undo functionality and the architecture's buffer modifications.

content.text:
Cancel rules are mostly correct in the whole Diacritics section. The key rule is sound:

> cancellation happens when the new input would produce the same letter diacritic already present on the immediate previous character.

That correctly separates cancel from override:

| Existing | Input | Meaning |
|---|---:|---|
| `Â` | `a/A` | cancel circumflex |
| `Â` | `w/W` | override to breve |
| `Ă` | `w/W` | cancel breve |
| `Ă` | `a/A` | override to circumflex |
| `Ô` | `o/O` | cancel circumflex |
| `Ô` | `w/W` | override to horn |
| `Ơ` | `w/W` | cancel horn |
| `Ơ` | `o/O` | override to circumflex |
| `Ư` | `w/W` | cancel horn |
| `Đ` | `d/D` | cancel bar/stroke |

Gaps or ambiguities I see:

1. **Literal mode reset is underspecified**

   This line is semantically important but vague:

   > Literal input remains until the process is reset (usually when starting new word).

   If this is implementation-facing, define what resets literal mode. For example:

   - whitespace?
   - punctuation?
   - cursor movement?
   - explicit buffer reset?
   - backspacing before the literal point?
   - committing the current word?

   “Usually when starting new word” is enough conceptually, but not precise enough for deterministic behavior.

2. **Backspace behavior needs a precise boundary**

   Current text:

   > When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.

   This likely means: after deleting the literal character that caused cancellation, the input method leaves literal mode.

   But the exact point should be defined. Example:

   - Existing `Â`, input `a` → `Aa`
   - Literal point is before or at the second `a`?
   - If user backspaces once, buffer becomes `A`.
   - Is Vietnamese mode active again immediately after that backspace?

   I think the intended rule is:

   > When backspace removes all characters at or after the literal point, switch back to Vietnamese input.

   Or more simply:

   > When backspace moves the cursor before the literal point, switch back to Vietnamese input.

   The current wording “pass before” should probably be “past” or “moves before”.

3. **Tone preservation is correct, but should imply tone normalization**

   This rule is correct:

   > The existing character keeps its case, tone, but the diacritic is removed.

   Examples:

   - `Ốo` → `Óo`
   - `Ắw` → `Áw`
   - `Ựw` → `Ụw`

   The important implied behavior is: when the letter diacritic is removed, the tone remains on the resulting base vowel if that base vowel can carry the tone. Your example `Ốo` → `Óo` communicates this well.

4. **`Đ/đ` has no tone interaction**

   “The existing character keeps its case, tone” is harmless for `Đ`, but `Đ` is a consonant and cannot have tone. No change required, but if being strict, tone preservation only matters for vowel diacritics.

5. **Input case is handled, but examples are incomplete**

   The rule says “ignore case,” so these should all cancel:

   - `Âa`, `ÂA` → `Aa`, `AA`
   - `âa`, `âA` → `aa`, `aA`
   - `Ưw`, `ƯW` → `Uw`, `UW`
   - `ưw`, `ưW` → `uw`, `uW`

   You do not need all examples, but the rule already supports them.

6. **“Immediate previous character” should mean the transformed character, not the literal base**

   This is probably clear from context, but worth keeping in mind: cancellation checks the immediate previous logical Vietnamese character/span, e.g. `Ố`, `Ắ`, `Đ`, not just raw ASCII input history.

Suggested minimal refinement:

```md
- The cancellation is triggered if and only if user inputs the character, ignoring case, that would
  produce the existing diacritic for the same base character of the immediate previous character.
- The existing character keeps its case and tone, but the letter diacritic is removed.
- The new character is literal, keeps its case, and has no tone.
- Switch to literal input starting from the new character position. Literal input remains until the
  process is reset.
- When backspace moves the cursor before the literal point, switch back to Vietnamese input.
```

Main gap: define **what resets literal mode** besides backspace. Everything else is logically consistent with apply and override rules.
Those gaps are acceptable. I updated the file. Now review the Diacritic auto-fill rules. Incomplete examples are acceptable. Consider the rules with the whole Diacritics section.

## Vietnamese Telex Rules

#### Circumflex

#### Breve

#### Horn

- Valid for: `O` (Ơ), `U` (Ư) and their lower cases.

#### Bar

#### Apply rules

- Only apply diacritic when the immediate previous character doesn't have any diacritic (empty).
- _Circumflex_, _bar_ are triggered when user inputs the same character (ignore case) with the
  existing character when that character is valid for the applying diacritic.
  - `Aa` -> `Â`, `Ee` -> `Ê`, `Oo` -> `Ô`.
  - `Dd` -> `Đ`.
- _Breve_, _horn_ are triggered when user input `W`, `w` after the character when that character is

  - `Aw` -> `Ă`.
  - `Ow` -> `Ơ`, `Uw` -> `Ư`.

- Treat out of scope characters as literal.

#### Override rules

- New valid diacritic can override existing valid diacritic of the same base character.
  - `Ăa` -> `Â`, `Âw` -> `Ă`.
  - `Ôw` -> `Ơ`, `Ơo` -> `Ô`.
- Only `A`, `O` have override rules because they have more than one possible letter diacritic.
- Keep tone untouched.
- The existing character keep its case, tone.
- Treat out of scope characters as literal.

#### Cancel rules

- The cancellation is triggered if and only if user inputs the character (ignore case) that would
  produce the existing diacritic for the same base character of the immediate previous character.
- The existing character keeps its case, tone, but the diacritic is removed.
  - Circumflex: `Âa` -> `Aa`, `Ốo` -> `Óo`... and so on for other cases of the same kind.
  - Breve: `Ăw` -> `Aw`.
  - Horn: `Ưw` -> `Uw`... and so on for other cases of the same kind.
  - Bar: `Đd` -> `Dd`, `đD` -> `dD`.
- The new character is literal, keeps its case, no tone.
- Switch to literal from the new character position and the following characters. Literal input
  remains until the process is reset (usually when starting new word).
  - Existing `Â`, input `aa`, result `Aaa`.
- When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.

#### Auto-fill rules

- The auto-fill is only applied for `ươ` syllable immediately before the user input, when one of the
  character in the syllable is missing horn: `uơ` and `ưo`.
- Fill the missing _horn_ when the input is one of these character (ignore case): `C`, `I`, `M`,
  `N`, `P`, `T`, `U` and their lower cases.
  - Fill missing horn on `U`: `uơn` -> `ươn`.
  - Fill missing horn on `O`: `ưóp` -> `ướp`.
- The new input character is appended literally.
- Tone and case of existing characters are preserved on the original characters.

### Tones

content.text:
Auto-fill rules are mostly coherent with the rest of the Diacritics section. The intent is clear: when the user has partially formed the `ươ` vowel cluster as either `uơ` or `ưo`, certain following letters imply the full cluster should become `ươ`.

Valid target syllable/rime cases are:

| Partial form | Trigger | Resulting pattern |
|---|---:|---|
| `uơ` | `c` | `ươc` |
| `uơ` | `i` | `ươi` |
| `uơ` | `m` | `ươm` |
| `uơ` | `n` | `ươn`, and later possibly `ương` if `g` follows |
| `uơ` | `p` | `ươp` |
| `uơ` | `t` | `ươt` |
| `uơ` | `u` | `ươu` |
| `ưo` | same triggers | same full `ươ...` forms |

No major missing case if `n` is intended to cover the start of `ng` in `ương`.

Gaps or ambiguities:

1. **The rule says “`ươ` syllable” but examples are rimes/clusters**

   This is not strictly a full syllable; it is the vowel cluster/rime immediately before the new input. Better wording would be:

   > Auto-fill is only applied to a partial `ươ` vowel cluster immediately before the user input...

2. **`n` trigger and `ương` should be intentional**

   Your trigger list includes `N`, which allows:

   - `uơn` → `ươn`
   - then later `g` gives `ương`

   That is fine, but it also means auto-fill happens before knowing whether the final is `n` or `ng`. This is probably correct because both `ươn` and `ương` are valid Vietnamese rimes.

3. **The examples should avoid tone confusion**

   This example is potentially confusing:

   > `ưóp` -> `ướp`

   It includes a tone change/placement effect: `ó` becomes `ớ`. Since this section says tones are separate, the example is valid only if the existing tone is preserved while changing `o/ó` to `ơ/ớ`.

   It might be clearer as:

   - `ưop` -> `ươp`
   - `ưóp` -> `ướp` for tone-preservation example

   The rule itself already says tone is preserved, so this is not wrong.

4. **Case preservation across two-character cluster should be explicit**

   You say case of existing characters is preserved. That implies:

   - `uƠn` → `ưƠn`
   - `Ưop` → `Ươp`
   - `Uơn` → `Ươn`
   - `ưOn` → `ươn` or `ưƠn` depending on existing `O` case

   This is correct if each original character keeps its own case. If instead Vietnamese output should normalize the second letter based on original case, say so. Current wording implies per-character preservation.

5. **Auto-fill priority relative to cancel/override is implicit**

   In the whole Diacritics section, auto-fill is not a direct transformation of the immediate previous character only; it transforms the previous two-character cluster. That is fine, but implementation priority should be clear:

   - apply/override/cancel operate on the immediate previous character.
   - auto-fill operates on the immediate previous partial cluster plus the new literal input.

   No contradiction, but it is a distinct rule shape.

6. **Literal mode after cancel should probably suppress auto-fill**

   Given cancel rules say “switch to literal from the new character position and following characters,” auto-fill should not run while in literal mode. This follows from the cancel section, but it may be worth making explicit if auto-fill is implemented separately.

Suggested tightened wording:

```md
#### Auto-fill rules

- Auto-fill is only applied to a partial `ươ` vowel cluster immediately before the user input, when
  exactly one character in the cluster is missing horn: `uơ` or `ưo`.
- Fill the missing _horn_ when the input is one of these characters, ignoring case: `C`, `I`, `M`,
  `N`, `P`, `T`, `U`.
  - Fill missing horn on `U`: `uơn` -> `ươn`.
  - Fill missing horn on `O`: `ưop` -> `ươp`.
- The new input character is appended literally.
- Tone and case of existing characters are preserved on their original characters.
```

Main thing to check: if you intentionally want to support `ương`, the `N` trigger is enough because `ng` starts with `n`; you do not need a separate `G` trigger.
